## DISTRIBUTIONAL SLICED-WASSERSTEIN AND APPLICATIONS TO GENERATIVE MODELING

## **Khai Nguyen**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.khainb@vinai.io

## **Nhat Ho** _[∗]_

University of Texas, Austin VinAI Research, Vietnam minhnhat@utexas.edu

## **Tung Pham**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.tungph4@vinai.io

## **Hung Bui**

VinAI Research, Vietnam v.hungbh1@vinai.io

## ABSTRACT

Sliced-Wasserstein distance (SW) and its variant, Max Sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-SW), have been used widely in the recent years due to their fast computation and scalability even when the probability measures lie in a very high dimensional space. However, SW requires many unnecessary projection samples to approximate its value while Max-SW only uses the most important projection, which ignores the information of other useful directions. In order to account for these weaknesses, we propose a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein_ distance (DSW), that finds an _optimal_ distribution over projections that can balance between exploring distinctive projecting directions and the informativeness of projections themselves. We show that the DSW is a generalization of Max-SW, and it can be computed efficiently by searching for the optimal push-forward measure over a set of probability measures over the unit sphere satisfying certain regularizing constraints that favor distinct directions. Finally, we conduct extensive experiments with large-scale datasets to demonstrate the favorable performances of the proposed distances over the previous sliced-based distances in generative modeling applications.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Optimal transport (OT) is a classical problem in mathematics and operation research. Due to its appealing theoretical properties and flexibility in practical applications, it has recently become an important tool in the machine learning and statistics community; see for example, (Courty et al., 2017; Arjovsky et al., 2017; Tolstikhin et al., 2018; Gulrajani et al., 2017) and references therein. The main usage of OT is to provide a distance named Wasserstein distance, to measure the discrepancy between two probability distributions. However, that distance suffers from expensive computational complexity, which is the main obstacle to using OT in practical applications.

There have been two main approaches to overcome the high computational complexity problem: either approximate the value of OT or apply the OT adaptively to specific situations. The first approach was initiated by (Cuturi, 2013) using an entropic regularizer to speed up the computation of the OT (Sinkhorn, 1967; Knight, 2008). The entropic regularization approach has demonstrated its usefulness in several application domains (Courty et al., 2014; Genevay et al., 2018; Bunne et al., 2019). Along this direction, several works proposed efficient algorithms for solving the entropic OT (Altschuler et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2019b;a) as well as methods to stabilize these algorithms (Chizat et al., 2018; Peyré & Cuturi, 2019; Chizat et al., 2018; Schmitzer, 2019). However, these algorithms have complexities of the order _O_ ( _k_[2] ), where _k_ is the number of supports. It is expensive when we need to compute the OT repeatedly, especially in learning the data distribution.

The second approach, known as "slicing", takes a rather different perspective. It leverages two key ideas: the OT closed-form expression for two distributions in one-dimensional space, and the transformation of a distribution into a set of projected one-dimensional distributions by the Radon transform (RT) (Helgason, 2010). The popular proposal along this direction is Sliced-Wasserstein (SW) distance (Bonneel et al., 2015), which samples the projecting directions uniformly over a unit sphere in the data ambient space and takes the expectation of the resulting one-dimensional OT distance. The SW distance hence requires a significantly lower computation cost than the original Wasserstein distance and is more scalable than the first approach. Due to its solid statistical guarantees and efficient computation, the SW distance has been successfully applied to a variety of practical tasks (Deshpande et al., 2018; Liutkus et al., 2019; Kolouri et al., 2018; Wu et al., 2019; Deshpande et al., 2019) where it has been shown to have comparative performances to other distances and divergences between probability distributions. However, there is an inevitable bottleneck of computing the SW distance. Specifically, the expectation with respect to the uniform distribution of projections in SW is intractable to compute; therefore, the Monte Carlo method is employed to approximate it. Nevertheless, drawing from a uniform distribution of directions in high-dimension can result in an overwhelming number of irrelevant directions, especially when the actual data lies in a low-dimensional manifold. Hence, SW typically needs to have a large number of samples to yield an accurate estimation of the discrepancy. Alternatively, in the other extreme, Max Sliced-Wasserstein (Max-SW) distance (Deshpande et al., 2019) uses only one important direction to distinguish the probability distributions. However, other potentially relevant directions are ignored in Max-SW. Therefore, Max-SW can miss some important differences between the two distributions in high dimension. We note that the linear projections in the Radon transform can be replaced by non-linear projections resulting in the generalized sliced-Wasserstein distance and its variants (Beylkin, 1984; Kolouri et al., 2019).

Apart from these main directions, there are also few proposals that try either to modify them or to combine the advantages of the above-mentioned approaches. In particular, Paty & Cuturi (2019) extended the idea of the max-sliced distance to the max-subspace distance by considering finding an optimal orthogonal subspace. However, this approach is computationally expensive, since it could not exploit the closed-form of the one-dimensional Wasserstein distance. Another approach named the Projected Wasserstein distance (PWD), which was proposed in (Rowland et al., 2019), uses sliced decomposition to find multiple one-dimension optimal transport maps. Then, it computes the average cost of those maps equally in the original dimension.

**Our contributions.** Our paper also follows the slicing approach. However, we address key friction in this general line of work: how to obtain a relatively small number of slices simultaneously to maintain the computational efficiency, but at the same time, cover the major differences between two high-dimensional distributions. We take a probabilistic view of slicing by using a probability measure on the unit sphere to represent how important each direction is. From this viewpoint, SW uses the uniform distribution while Max-SW searches for the best delta-Dirac distribution over the projections, both can be considered as special cases. In this paper, we propose to search for an optimal distribution of important directions. We regularize this distribution such that it prefers directions that are far away from one another, hence encouraging an efficient exploration of the space of directions. In the case of no regularization, our proposed method recovers max-(generalized) SW as a special case. In summary, our main contributions are two-fold:

1. First, we introduce a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein distance_ (DSW), to account for the issues of previous sliced distances. Our main idea is to search for not just a single most important projection, but an _optimal_ distribution over projections that could balance between an expansion of the area around important projections and the informativeness of projections themselves, i.e., how well they can distinguish the two target probability measures. We show that DSW is a proper metric in the probability space and possesses appealing statistical and computational properties as the previous sliced distances.

2. Second, we apply the DSW distance to generative modeling tasks based on the generative adversarial framework. The extensive experiments on real and large-scale datasets show that DSW distance significantly outperforms the SW and Max-SW distances under similar computational time on these tasks. Furthermore, the DSW distance helps model distribution converge to the data distribution faster and provides more realistic generated images than the SW and Max-SW distances.

**Organization.** The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we provide backgrounds for Wasserstein distance and its slice-based versions. In Section 3, we propose distributional (generalized) sliced-Wasserstein distance and analyze some of its theoretical properties. Section 4 includes extensive experiment results followed by discussions in Section 5. Finally, we defer the proofs of key results and extra materials in the Appendices.

However, important directions are not distributed uniformly over the sphere. Thus, this approach will draw potentially many unimportant projections that are not only expensive but also greatly reduce the effect of the SW distance.

## 2.4 MAX (GENERALIZED) SLICED-WASSERSTEIN DISTANCES

An approach to using only informative directions is to simply take the best slice in discriminating two given probability distributions. That distance is max sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-SW) (Deshpande et al., 2019), which is given by max _SWp_ ( _µ, ν_ ) := max _θ∈_ S _d−_ 1 _Wp_ ( _RIµ_ ( _·, θ_ ) _, RIν_ ( _·, θ_ )). By combining this idea with non-linear projections from generalized Radon transform, we obtain max generalized sliced-Wasserstein distance (Max-GSW) (Kolouri et al., 2019). The formal definition of that distance is: max _GSWp_ ( _µ, ν_ ) := max _θ∈_ Ω _θ Wp_ ( _GIµ_ ( _·, θ_ ) _, GIν_ ( _·, θ_ )). The (generalized) MaxSW distances focus on finding only the most important direction. Meanwhile, other informative directions play no role in the distance. Therefore, (generalized) Max-SW distances can ignore useful information about the structure of high dimensional probability measures.

## 3 DISTRIBUTIONAL SLICED-WASSERSTEIN DISTANCE

With the aim of improving the limitations of the previous sliced distances, we propose a novel distance, named _Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein distance_ (DSW), that can search for not just a single but a distribution of important directions on the unit sphere. We prove that it is a well-defined metric and discuss its connection to the existing sliced-based distances in Section 3.1. Then, we provide a procedure to approximate DSW based on its dual form in Section 3.2.

## 3.1 DEFINITION AND METRICITY

We first start with a definition of distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance. We say _C >_ 0 _admissible_ if the set M _C_ of probability measures _σ_ on S _[d][−]_[1] satisfying E _θ,θ′∼σ_ � _|θ[⊤] θ[′] |_ � _≤ C_ is not empty.

**Definition 1.** _Given two probability measures µ and ν on_ R _[d] with finite p-th moments where p ≥_ 1 _and an admissible regularizing constant C >_ 0 _. The_ distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance _(DSW) of order p between µ and ν is given by:_

_where R is the Radon transform operator._

The DSW aims to find the optimal probability measure of slices on the unit sphere S _[d][−]_[1] . Note that, the Max-SW distance is equivalent to searching for the best Dirac measure on a single point in S _[d][−]_[1] , which puts all weights in only one direction. Meanwhile, the uniform measure in the formulation of SW distance distributes the same weights in all directions. Indeed, the uniform and Dirac measures are two special cases, because they view that either all directions are equally important or only one direction is important. That view is too restricted if the data actually lie on low dimensional space. Thus, we aim to find a probability measure which concentrates only on areas around important directions. Furthermore, we do not want these directions to lie in only one small area, because under the orthogonal projection of RT, their corresponding one-dimensional distributions will become similar. In order to achieve this, we search for an optimal measure _σ_ that satisfies the regularization constraint E _θ,θ[′] ∼σ_ [�� _θ⊤θ′_[�] �] _≤ C_ . By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, _C_ is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere. Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure corresponding to the Max-SW distance. When _C_ is small, the constraint forces the measure _σ_ to distribute more weights to directions that are far from each other (in terms of their angles). Thus, a small appropriate value of _C_ will help to balance between the distinctiveness and informativeness of these targeted directions. For further discussion about _C_ , see Appendix B.1. Next, we show that DSW is a well-defined metric on the probability space. **Theorem 1.** _For any p ≥_ 1 _and admissible C >_ 0 _, DSWp_ ( _·, ·_ ; _C_ ) _is a well-defined metric in the space of Borel probability measures with finite p-th moment. In particular, it is non-negative, symmetric, identity, and satisfies the triangle inequality._

As a consequence of Theorem 2, the statistical error of estimating the unknown distribution based on the empirical distribution of _n_ i.i.d data under DSW distance is _Cd · n[−]_[1] _[/]_[2] with high probability where _Cd_ is some universal constant depending on dimension _d_ (see Appendix B.3). Therefore, as other sliced-based Wasserstein distances, the DSW distance does not suffer from the curse of dimensionality.

**Finding the optimal** _f_ **:** We parameterize _f_ in the dual form (2) by using a deep neural network with parameter _φ_ , defined as _fφ_ . Then, we estimate the gradient of the objective function DS( _fφ_ ) in equation (2) with respect to _φ_ and use stochastic gradient ascent algorithm to update _φ_ . Since there are expectations over uniform distribution in the gradient of DS( _fφ_ ), we use the Monte Carlo method to approximate these expectations. Note that, we can use the fixed point from the stochastic ascent algorithm to approximate the dual value of DSW in equation (2). A detailed argument for this point is in Appendix B.2. Finally, in generative model applications with DSW being the loss function, we only need to use the gradient of the function DS( _._ ) to update the parameters of interest. Therefore, we can treat _λC_ as a regularized parameter and tune it to find suitable value in these applications.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we conduct extensive experiments comparing the performance in both generative quality and computational speed of the proposed DSW distance with other sliced-based distances, namely the SW, Max-SW, Max-GSW-NN (Kolouri et al., 2019) and projected robust subspace Wasserstein (PRW) (Paty & Cuturi, 2019; Lin et al., 2020) using the minimum expected distance estimator (MEDE) (Bernton et al., 2019) on MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998), CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky, 2009), CelebA (Liu et al., 2015) and LSUN (Yu et al., 2015) datasets. The details of the MEDE framework are described in Appendix D. We would like to note that the wall-clock timing of different methods may be subject to the differences in the hyperparameter settings and software implementations of different methods. On MNIST dataset, we train generative models with different distances and then evaluate their performances by comparing Wasserstein-2 distances between 10000 random generated images and all images from the MNIST test set. Due to the very large size of other datasets, e.g., 3 million images in LSUN, it is expensive to compute empirical Wasserstein-2 distance as its complexity is of order _O_ ( _k_[2] log _k_ ) where _k_ is the number of support points. Therefore, after we train generative models, we use FID score (Heusel et al., 2017) to evaluate the generative quality of these generators. The FID score is calculated from 10000 random generated images and all training samples using precomputed statistics in (Heusel et al., 2017). Finally, for _λC_ in DSW (see Definition 2), it is chosen in the set _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 100 _,_ 1000 _}_ such that its Wasserstein-2 (FID score) (between 10000 random generated images and all images from corresponding validation set) is the lowest among the four values. Detailed experiment settings are in Appendix G. Finally, we also apply the DSW into color transfer task (Rabin et al., 2010; 2014; Bonneel et al., 2015; Perrot et al., 2016) in Appendix F, where we find that DSW also performs better than SW and Max-SW in this task.

Then we measure the computational time per minibatch and the Wasserstein-2 score of the learned generators for each _N_ . We plot the Wasserstein-2 score and computational time of Max-SW and Max-GSW-NN in their standard settings (Kolouri et al., 2019). Except for the regime with very fast but low-quality learned models, DSW is better than all the existing slice-based baselines in terms of both model quality and computational speed. Moreover, DSW can learn good models with very few projections, e.g., DSW-10 achieves better model quality than Max-GSW-NN and Max-SW and is one order-of-magnitude faster than these sliced distances. Finally, with a similar computational time, a learned generator by DSW has the Wasserstein-2 score that is roughly 10% lower than the one got from SW. For the qualitative comparison between these distances, we show random generated images from their generative models in Figure 7 in Appendix E.1. We observe that generated images from DSW are sharper and easier to classify into numbers than those from other baseline distances.

**Comparison with projected robust subspace Wasserstein (PRW) and Wasserstein distance:** In Figure 2(a), we plot the Wasserstein-2 score and computational time of Wasserstein distance (WD) and PRW, where the subspace dimension of PRW varies in the range _{_ 2 _,_ 5 _,_ 10 _,_ 50 _}_ . PRW is able to improve upon the model quality of slice-based methods including DSW, however at the cost of being an order of magnitude slower than DSW with 10 projections (DSW-10). We observe that DSW-10 obtains a better Wasserstein-2 score than PRW with 5-dimensional subspace, while its corresponding computational time is 30 times faster than that of PRW-5. Using 50 dimension, PRW’s Wasserstein-2 score improves about 29% to that of DSW-10 but the computational cost is also around 40 times slower. The model trained by WD gives good Wasserstein-2 score; however, it is computational expensive (about 40 times slower than DSW-10). The main computational advantage of DSW comes from the exact calculation of Wasserstein distance in one-dimension. The visual comparison between PRW, WD and DSW based on their generated images is in Figure 12 in Appendix E.2.

**Convergence behavior:** Figure 2(b) shows that DSW learns better models at a faster speed of convergence than other baseline distances with a very small number of projections, e.g., DSW-10 is the second lowest curve compared to curves from other sliced-based distances.

Furthermore, we carry out experiments with DGSW, an extension of DSW to non-linear projections, and test the new proposed distances in training encoder-generator models on MNIST using joint contrastive inference (JCI) in Appendices E.1 and E.3. ## 4.2 RESULTS ON LARGE-SCALE DATASETS

Next, we conduct large-scale experiments on a range of more realistic image datasets. We train generative models using CIFAR10, CelebA, and LSUN datasets (all these datasets are rescaled to 64x64 resolution). When working with high dimensional distributions, Deshpande et al. (2018) proposed a trick to improve the quality of the generator by learning a feature function which maps data to a new feature space that is more manageable in size. When the feature function is fixed, the generator is trained to match the distribution of features. When the generator is fixed, the feature function tries to tease apart the data empirical features from the generated feature distribution. For the experiments in this section, we use the same technique with DSW and all other baseline distances.

We compare DSW with SW, Max-SW, and Max-GSW-NN in both generative quality (FID score) and computational time in Figure 3. We could not compare DSW with PRW on the large-scale datasets since PRW is computationally expensive to train to obtain good generated images. On CelebA and CIFAR10, we let _N_ , the number of projections of both DSW and SW, vary in the set _{_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _,_ 10[4] _}_ . For LSUN, since it takes considerably longer time to train each model, we only vary _N_ in the set _{_ 10[2] _,_ 10[4] _}_ . On all these large datasets, DSW outperforms all the other baselines in both FID score of the learned model and computational efficiency. The gap of FID scores between DSW and other methods is especially large on CIFAR10 and LSUN. For example, on CIFAR10, with the same computational time, FID scores of DSW are always lower than those of SW about 20 units. On LSUN, with 100 projections, DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60. It is interesting to note that on these high-dimensional datasets, Max-SW performs rather poorly: it obtains the highest FID scores among all distances while requires heavy computation. Max-GSW-NN has better FID scores than (Max)-SW; however, it is still worse than DSW and while being slower. This is consistent with the intuition that as the number of dimension of the data grows, the use of a single important slice in Max-SW becomes a less efficient approximation. DSW, on the other hand, is able to make use of more important slices, and at the same time avoids SW’s inefficiency of uniform slice-sampling.

Generated images from CelebA, CIFAR10 and LSUN are deferred to Appendix E.1. Comparing to other sliced-based Wasserstein distances, generated samples obtained from the DSW’s generative model are also more visually realistic. Further experiments to compare DGSW with GSW, Max-GSW, and Max-GSW-NN are also given in the Appendix E.1. Based on these experiments, we can conclude that the distributional approach also improves the generative quality of non-linear slicing distances.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we have presented the novel distributional sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distances between two probability measures. Our main idea is to search for the best distribution of important directions while regularizing towards orthogonal directions. We prove that they are well-defined metrics and provide their theoretical and computational properties. We compare our proposed distances to other sliced-based distances in a variety of generative modeling tasks, including estimating generative models and jointly estimating both generators and inference models. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our new distances yield significantly better models and convergence behaviors during training than the previous sliced-based distances. One important future direction is to investigate theoretically the optimal choice of the regularization parameter _λC_ such that the DSW distance can capture all the important directions that can distinguish two target probability measures well.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper introduces a well-motivated and conceptually sound advancement in the field of optimal transport: the Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distance. By framing the selection of projection directions as a search for an optimal probability measure rather than relying on uniform sampling (SW) or a singular direction (Max-SW), the authors successfully identify a clear gap in existing literature. The introduction is particularly strong, providing a logical narrative flow that situates the proposed method within the context of trade-offs between computational efficiency and descriptive accuracy. The writing is clear, the problem space is well-defined, and the technical motivation—using regularization to encourage distinct, informative projection directions—is both intuitive and elegant. The most significant challenge for the remainder of the paper will be to rigorously demonstrate that the DSW can be optimized computationally without succumbing to the "curse of dimensionality" or the overhead that the authors explicitly seek to avoid in competing methods like Max-subspace. Provided the empirical results substantiate these theoretical claims, this work represents a substantial and coherent contribution to generative modeling.

Even accounting for OCR, this is a significant disruption in the definition of a primary geometric object used for the regularization term.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Admissible Constant C
comments.quote: We say C > 0 admissible if the set M C of probability measures \sigma on S {d−} 1 satisfying E {\theta,\theta[′]∼\sigma}  |\theta{⊤} \theta{′} |  \le C is not empty.
comments.explanation: The term $|\theta^\top \theta'|$ is used as the random variable inside the expectation, but the regularization term in Definition 2 is later described as $\mathbb{E}_{\theta, \theta' \sim \sigma} [\| \theta^\top \theta' \|]$. Given that $\theta, \theta' \in S^{d-1}$, their inner product can naturally be negative. If the intent is an anti-alignment constraint (to favor distinct directions), placing an expectation over the absolute value makes physical sense, but if it is over the raw inner product, the constraint functions differently (favoring orthogonality). The text is inconsistent between the definition of the set $M_C$ and the dual form description, creating uncertainty about whether the model penalizes parallelism or just expects low average inner product.

comments.title: Implicit Dependency on Parameter \lambda_C
comments.quote: In Definition 2, the set M C disappears and \lambda_C plays the tuning role for the regularized term E {\theta,\theta[′]∼\sigma}  |\theta{⊤} \theta{′} |  .
comments.explanation: The authors refer to a constant $\lambda_C$ 'depending on $C$' but do not define the functional relationship between $\lambda_C$ and $C$ (e.g., via a Legendre-Fenchel transform). While they state later that $\lambda_C$ can be treated as a regularization parameter to be tuned, the lack of a explicit derivation for $\lambda_C$ makes the transition from the constrained optimization problem to the unconstrained Lagrangian form less rigorous than what is typically expected for a theoretical paper.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Definition of Admissibility
comments.quote: We say $C > 0$ $admissible$ if the set $\mathbb{M}_C$ of probability measures $\sigma$ on $S^{d-1}$ satisfying $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \leq C$ is not empty.
comments.explanation: The definition of admissibility requires the set $\mathbb{M}_C$ to be non-empty. However, for any distribution $\sigma$ on the sphere, $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \geq 0$. More importantly, considering the Dirac measure $\delta_{\theta_0}$, the expectation evaluates to $|\theta_0^{\top}\theta_0| = 1$. Thus, for any $C \geq 1$, $\mathbb{M}_C$ is guaranteed to be non-empty (as it contains all Dirac measures). The authors later state "By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $C$ is no greater than $1$", which contradicts the definition of admissibility provided here. If $C < 0$, the set is empty, but the phrasing "admissible if... is not empty" suggests that $C$ is a constraint parameter that might be chosen such that it becomes restrictive, not merely a binary validity condition for $C$.

comments.title: Ambiguous Mathematical Notation for Inner Product Expectation
comments.quote: satisfying $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \leq C$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1 and throughout, the authors define the regularization term using absolute value notation $|\theta^{\top}\theta'|$. In the text, they justify this via the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality implying $C \leq 1$. However, the standard Rayleigh quotient/correlation alignment often considers the squared inner product $(\theta^{\top}\theta')^2$ to avoid sign-flipping issues or simply to measure orthogonality (as in determinantal point processes or repulsion). Given the context of seeking 'distinct' directions, the absolute value is mathematically valid but atypical compared to the squared form in similar literature (e.g., repulsive processes). While this doesn't break the paper, the notation is slightly confusing given the later reference to the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which is traditionally applied to the square, i.e., $(\mathbb{E}[\theta^{\top}\theta'])^2 \leq \mathbb{E}[\|\theta\|^2]\mathbb{E}[\|\theta'\|^2] = 1$. Using the absolute value version doesn't change the bound to 1 directly without further derivation.

comments.title: Notation Clash: Function Argument Symbol $I$
comments.quote: we denote their corresponding probability density functions as $I_{\mu}$ and $I_{\nu}$
comments.explanation: The authors define $I_{\mu}$ as the probability density function of measure $\mu$. However, in Section 2.2, they define $I \in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)$ as a function being transformed by the Radon transform. It is standard to use $p$ or $f$ for densities; using $I$ for both a general function $I$ and as a prefix for density functions $I_{\mu}$ causes notational overlap that could confuse a reader trying to keep track of whether $I$ is an abstract input function or a density.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Dual Form Equation Reference
comments.quote: DSW_p(μ, ν; C) ≥ DSW_p^*(μ, ν; C)
comments.explanation: The text references a dual form in Definition 2 and subsequently defines a 'DSW^*' object. However, Definition 2 does not explicitly label its result as 'DSW^*'. The notation 'DSW^*' appears for the first time in the line 'DSW_p(μ, ν; C) ≥ DSW_p^*(μ, ν; C)'. While contextually it is clear that the authors are referring to the relaxation/dual form defined in Eq (2), the lack of a formal equation number or clear definition for the symbol 'DSW^*' itself creates a minor tracking issue for the reader.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of 'Admissible' Regularizing Constant C
comments.quote: Specifically, note that, the Max-SW distance is equivalent to searching for the best Dirac measure on a single point in S d−1 , which puts all weights in only one direction... By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere. Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure corresponding to the Max-SW distance.
comments.explanation: The definition provided is E[|θᵀθ'|] <= C. The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states |θᵀθ'| <= ||θ|| ||θ'||. Since θ and θ' are on the unit sphere, |θᵀθ'| <= 1. The expectation E[|θᵀθ'|] must therefore be <= 1. Setting C=1 effectively removes the constraint, allowing any distribution. The authors state 'M1 contains all probability measures', which is correct. However, they claim 'Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure'. This is not immediately obvious; optimizing over all probability measures for the 'most informative' projection usually yields a Dirac measure, but the relationship between the DSW objective and the Max-SW objective in this limit should be explicitly linked to the collapse of the distribution to a delta. The logic holds, but the justification is slightly hand-wavy given the complexity of the DSW objective.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of the 'Dual' Objective
comments.quote: For any p ≥ 1 and admissible C > 0, there exists a non-negative constant λC depending on C such that the dual form of DSW distance takes the following form
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the expression (Definition 2) as the 'dual form'. However, in standard constrained optimization, the expression provided (a sum of the original objective and a penalty term with λ) is a Lagrangian or a penalized objective, not necessarily a 'dual' in the strict sense of Fenchel or Wolfe duality. While common in machine learning literature, calling it the 'dual form' without showing the derivation from the primal constraint can be slightly misleading for a rigorous reader. It is technically a penalized version of the primal objective.

comments.title: Inconsistency in expectation notation for regularized term
comments.explanation: In Section 1 (Key Equations) and Definition 1, the regularization constraint is defined using the absolute value of the inner product: E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C. However, in the discussion of the 'Effect of the regularization parameter λC' in Section 4.1, the authors define a term AN = 1/N^2 * Σ_{i,j=1}^N |θ_i^⊤ θ_j|. While this converges to the expectation of the absolute value, they later equate this to the term in the dual form of the DSW in equation (2): 'an approximation of the regularized term E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] in the dual form of DSW'. In Definition 2, the dual form is written as: λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. The notation here is confusing; the expectation is taken over θ, θ' ~ σ_{d-1} (the uniform distribution), but the integrand uses f(θ) and f(θ'). This is mathematically distinct from the regularization term defined in the constraint E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C where σ is the target measure itself. While the pushforward mechanism justifies this transition, the text repeatedly swaps between E[|θ^⊤ θ'|] (integral over σ) and E[|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|] (integral over uniform σ_{d-1}), which might lead a reader to incorrectly assume these are identical parameters rather than a change of variables.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the objective function DS(f_φ)
comments.quote: DS( fφ ) in equation (2)
comments.explanation: The text refers to maximizing 'the objective function DS( fφ ) in equation (2)'. Looking at the Reparameterized Dual Form in the provided context (and Equation 2 mentioned in the paper), the objective is: sup_{f} E_{θ∼σ_{d-1}} [W_p^p(RI_μ(·, f(θ)), RI_ν(·, f(θ)))] - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. The authors state that they 'estimate the gradient of the objective function DS(f_φ)... and use stochastic gradient ascent'. This implies DS(f_φ) = E.W - λ_C * E.Penalty. However, if λ_C is defined as the inverse of C (as stated in section 1.1), the objective would actually be the Lagrangian of the DSW distance. If λ_C is treated as a tuning parameter, the optimization should technically be a min-max problem (as indicated by the 'sup_{σ}' in definition 1), but the authors treat it as an unconstrained maximization. While common in practice, the text conflates the dual relaxation with the original distance without explicitly clarifying the shift from a constrained optimization to a penalized one.

comments.title: Statisical error constant dependency
comments.explanation: The paper claims: 'the statistical error of estimating the unknown distribution... under DSW distance is C_d * n^{-1/2} with high probability'. In Theorem 2(b), it relates DSW to SW and Max-SW. The error of Monte Carlo estimation of sliced-based distances is notoriously affected by the dimension d. The claim that it 'does not suffer from the curse of dimensionality' (supported by invoking the statistical error of SW) is slightly misleading because, while it doesn't scale exponentially like standard Wasserstein, the constant C_d can depend on the dimension d in ways that make it behave poorly in extremely high dimensions (as noted by the authors' mention of the need for large N to define 'important' directions). Flagging as minor because the intuition is standard for this subfield, but technically imprecise regarding the constant behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Dual Form objective function index
comments.explanation: The reparameterized objective includes a term E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. In the notation list and throughout the paper, θ and θ' usually denote directions on the sphere S^{d-1}. When applying a reparameterization f (a neural network), the expectation should be over the base measure (often uniform on the sphere, denoted as σ_{d-1}). While the context clearly implies the intent is to compute the expectation over the uniform distribution on the sphere for both variables, the notation |f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')| inside the expectation is mathematically inconsistent because the expectation is indexed only by θ and θ'. A more precise notation would be E_{θ, θ' ∼ σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. As written, it uses the subscript σ_{d-1} once but looks as if it might imply independent samples from that measure. It is a minor notation ambiguity that a reader can resolve, but it is technically malformed.

comments.title: Implicit assumption on the domain of f
comments.quote: where f is a class of all Borel measurable functions from S^{d-1} to S^{d-1}
comments.explanation: The text states f is a 'class' of functions, but in the preceding formula (2), the supremum is taken over 'f'. If f is a class, the supremum should be over the functions within that class. The phrasing is slightly imprecise as it conflates the function itself with the space of functions. Given the subsequent sentence 'We parameterize f ... by using a deep neural network', the authors clearly mean the set of functions represented by the architecture. This is a common shortcut in ML papers, but slightly formally loose.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'admissible constant'
comments.explanation: In section Section 3.1, the authors define C > 0 as admissible if the set M_C is non-empty. Later, they state 'By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1'. While true for E[|θᵀθ'|], this assumes the measure σ is such that the expectation of the absolute inner product is bounded by the max of |θᵀθ'| (which is 1). This is trivially true, but the paper does not explicitly clarify if C can range up to 1 or if specific choices of C are required for specific d. It's a minor clarification point since the constraint E[|θᵀθ'|] ≤ C is always satisfied for C=1, but it might leave a user wondering what happens if they set C > 1.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Definition of Dual Form Regularization
comments.explanation: In Section 1 (Key Equations) and Section 3.1, the Alignment Regularization is defined as E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C. However, in Definition 2 (Section 3.2), the dual form introduces the term - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|]. The context states λC is equivalent to the inverse of C. In standard Lagrange multiplier theory for a constraint f(θ,θ') ≤ C, the penalty should be λ(E[|θ⊤θ'|] - C). By minimizing the negative of the objective (or maximizing a Lagrangian), the constant term λC*C would appear. The current formulation - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] omits the constant offset λC*C. While common in some machine learning literature for 'regularization', strictly speaking, it is not the dual form of the constrained problem defined in 3.1 unless the constant is accounted for or the definition of 'dual form' is interpreted loosely as a penalized objective.

comments.title: Notation confusion in Reparameterized Dual Form
comments.explanation: In the summary and Section 3.2, the index θ∼σ_{d-1} appears, where σ_{d-1} is defined in the summary as the 'uniform distribution'. However, the symbol σ_{d-1} is not clearly defined in the 'Notation' subsection of the text itself. While the context allows one to infer σ_{d-1} denotes the uniform measure on the sphere, the notation is inconsistent with the earlier defined σ (a probability measure to be optimized) and S^{d-1} (the unit sphere). This might confuse a reader whether σ_{d-1} refers to a distribution or the sphere itself. It is likely a typo for the uniform measure which should be denoted using the standard Haar measure notation or clearly mapped to S^{d-1}.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of Admissible Constant C
comments.quote: We say C > 0 admissible if the set M C of probability measures \sigma on S {d−} 1 satisfying E {\theta,\theta[′]∼\sigma}  |\theta{⊤} \theta{′} |  \le C is not empty.
comments.explanation: The term $|\theta^\top \theta'|$ is used as the random variable inside the expectation, but the regularization term in Definition 2 is later described as $\mathbb{E}_{\theta, \theta' \sim \sigma} [\| \theta^\top \theta' \|]$. Given that $\theta, \theta' \in S^{d-1}$, their inner product can naturally be negative. If the intent is an anti-alignment constraint (to favor distinct directions), placing an expectation over the absolute value makes physical sense, but if it is over the raw inner product, the constraint functions differently (favoring orthogonality). The text is inconsistent between the definition of the set $M_C$ and the dual form description, creating uncertainty about whether the model penalizes parallelism or just expects low average inner product.

comments.title: Implicit Dependency on Parameter \lambda_C
comments.quote: In Definition 2, the set M C disappears and \lambda_C plays the tuning role for the regularized term E {\theta,\theta[′]∼\sigma}  |\theta{⊤} \theta{′} |  .
comments.explanation: The authors refer to a constant $\lambda_C$ 'depending on $C$' but do not define the functional relationship between $\lambda_C$ and $C$ (e.g., via a Legendre-Fenchel transform). While they state later that $\lambda_C$ can be treated as a regularization parameter to be tuned, the lack of a explicit derivation for $\lambda_C$ makes the transition from the constrained optimization problem to the unconstrained Lagrangian form less rigorous than what is typically expected for a theoretical paper.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Definition of Admissibility
comments.quote: We say $C > 0$ $admissible$ if the set $\mathbb{M}_C$ of probability measures $\sigma$ on $S^{d-1}$ satisfying $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \leq C$ is not empty.
comments.explanation: The definition of admissibility requires the set $\mathbb{M}_C$ to be non-empty. However, for any distribution $\sigma$ on the sphere, $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \geq 0$. More importantly, considering the Dirac measure $\delta_{\theta_0}$, the expectation evaluates to $|\theta_0^{\top}\theta_0| = 1$. Thus, for any $C \geq 1$, $\mathbb{M}_C$ is guaranteed to be non-empty (as it contains all Dirac measures). The authors later state "By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, $C$ is no greater than $1$", which contradicts the definition of admissibility provided here. If $C < 0$, the set is empty, but the phrasing "admissible if... is not empty" suggests that $C$ is a constraint parameter that might be chosen such that it becomes restrictive, not merely a binary validity condition for $C$.

comments.title: Ambiguous Mathematical Notation for Inner Product Expectation
comments.quote: satisfying $\mathbb{E}_{\theta,\theta'\sim\sigma} [|\theta^{\top}\theta'|] \leq C$
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1 and throughout, the authors define the regularization term using absolute value notation $|\theta^{\top}\theta'|$. In the text, they justify this via the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality implying $C \leq 1$. However, the standard Rayleigh quotient/correlation alignment often considers the squared inner product $(\theta^{\top}\theta')^2$ to avoid sign-flipping issues or simply to measure orthogonality (as in determinantal point processes or repulsion). Given the context of seeking 'distinct' directions, the absolute value is mathematically valid but atypical compared to the squared form in similar literature (e.g., repulsive processes). While this doesn't break the paper, the notation is slightly confusing given the later reference to the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, which is traditionally applied to the square, i.e., $(\mathbb{E}[\theta^{\top}\theta'])^2 \leq \mathbb{E}[\|\theta\|^2]\mathbb{E}[\|\theta'\|^2] = 1$. Using the absolute value version doesn't change the bound to 1 directly without further derivation.

comments.title: Notation Clash: Function Argument Symbol $I$
comments.quote: we denote their corresponding probability density functions as $I_{\mu}$ and $I_{\nu}$
comments.explanation: The authors define $I_{\mu}$ as the probability density function of measure $\mu$. However, in Section 2.2, they define $I \in L_1(\mathbb{R}^d)$ as a function being transformed by the Radon transform. It is standard to use $p$ or $f$ for densities; using $I$ for both a general function $I$ and as a prefix for density functions $I_{\mu}$ causes notational overlap that could confuse a reader trying to keep track of whether $I$ is an abstract input function or a density.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Dual Form Equation Reference
comments.quote: DSW_p(μ, ν; C) ≥ DSW_p^*(μ, ν; C)
comments.explanation: The text references a dual form in Definition 2 and subsequently defines a 'DSW^*' object. However, Definition 2 does not explicitly label its result as 'DSW^*'. The notation 'DSW^*' appears for the first time in the line 'DSW_p(μ, ν; C) ≥ DSW_p^*(μ, ν; C)'. While contextually it is clear that the authors are referring to the relaxation/dual form defined in Eq (2), the lack of a formal equation number or clear definition for the symbol 'DSW^*' itself creates a minor tracking issue for the reader.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of 'Admissible' Regularizing Constant C
comments.quote: Specifically, note that, the Max-SW distance is equivalent to searching for the best Dirac measure on a single point in S d−1 , which puts all weights in only one direction... By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere. Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure corresponding to the Max-SW distance.
comments.explanation: The definition provided is E[|θᵀθ'|] <= C. The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states |θᵀθ'| <= ||θ|| ||θ'||. Since θ and θ' are on the unit sphere, |θᵀθ'| <= 1. The expectation E[|θᵀθ'|] must therefore be <= 1. Setting C=1 effectively removes the constraint, allowing any distribution. The authors state 'M1 contains all probability measures', which is correct. However, they claim 'Optimizing over M1 simply returns the best Dirac measure'. This is not immediately obvious; optimizing over all probability measures for the 'most informative' projection usually yields a Dirac measure, but the relationship between the DSW objective and the Max-SW objective in this limit should be explicitly linked to the collapse of the distribution to a delta. The logic holds, but the justification is slightly hand-wavy given the complexity of the DSW objective.

comments.title: Ambiguous Definition of the 'Dual' Objective
comments.quote: For any p ≥ 1 and admissible C > 0, there exists a non-negative constant λC depending on C such that the dual form of DSW distance takes the following form
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the expression (Definition 2) as the 'dual form'. However, in standard constrained optimization, the expression provided (a sum of the original objective and a penalty term with λ) is a Lagrangian or a penalized objective, not necessarily a 'dual' in the strict sense of Fenchel or Wolfe duality. While common in machine learning literature, calling it the 'dual form' without showing the derivation from the primal constraint can be slightly misleading for a rigorous reader. It is technically a penalized version of the primal objective.

comments.title: Inconsistency in expectation notation for regularized term
comments.explanation: In Section 1 (Key Equations) and Definition 1, the regularization constraint is defined using the absolute value of the inner product: E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C. However, in the discussion of the 'Effect of the regularization parameter λC' in Section 4.1, the authors define a term AN = 1/N^2 * Σ_{i,j=1}^N |θ_i^⊤ θ_j|. While this converges to the expectation of the absolute value, they later equate this to the term in the dual form of the DSW in equation (2): 'an approximation of the regularized term E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] in the dual form of DSW'. In Definition 2, the dual form is written as: λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. The notation here is confusing; the expectation is taken over θ, θ' ~ σ_{d-1} (the uniform distribution), but the integrand uses f(θ) and f(θ'). This is mathematically distinct from the regularization term defined in the constraint E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C where σ is the target measure itself. While the pushforward mechanism justifies this transition, the text repeatedly swaps between E[|θ^⊤ θ'|] (integral over σ) and E[|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|] (integral over uniform σ_{d-1}), which might lead a reader to incorrectly assume these are identical parameters rather than a change of variables.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the objective function DS(f_φ)
comments.quote: DS( fφ ) in equation (2)
comments.explanation: The text refers to maximizing 'the objective function DS( fφ ) in equation (2)'. Looking at the Reparameterized Dual Form in the provided context (and Equation 2 mentioned in the paper), the objective is: sup_{f} E_{θ∼σ_{d-1}} [W_p^p(RI_μ(·, f(θ)), RI_ν(·, f(θ)))] - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. The authors state that they 'estimate the gradient of the objective function DS(f_φ)... and use stochastic gradient ascent'. This implies DS(f_φ) = E.W - λ_C * E.Penalty. However, if λ_C is defined as the inverse of C (as stated in section 1.1), the objective would actually be the Lagrangian of the DSW distance. If λ_C is treated as a tuning parameter, the optimization should technically be a min-max problem (as indicated by the 'sup_{σ}' in definition 1), but the authors treat it as an unconstrained maximization. While common in practice, the text conflates the dual relaxation with the original distance without explicitly clarifying the shift from a constrained optimization to a penalized one.

comments.title: Statisical error constant dependency
comments.explanation: The paper claims: 'the statistical error of estimating the unknown distribution... under DSW distance is C_d * n^{-1/2} with high probability'. In Theorem 2(b), it relates DSW to SW and Max-SW. The error of Monte Carlo estimation of sliced-based distances is notoriously affected by the dimension d. The claim that it 'does not suffer from the curse of dimensionality' (supported by invoking the statistical error of SW) is slightly misleading because, while it doesn't scale exponentially like standard Wasserstein, the constant C_d can depend on the dimension d in ways that make it behave poorly in extremely high dimensions (as noted by the authors' mention of the need for large N to define 'important' directions). Flagging as minor because the intuition is standard for this subfield, but technically imprecise regarding the constant behavior.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Dual Form objective function index
comments.explanation: The reparameterized objective includes a term E_{θ,θ'∼σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. In the notation list and throughout the paper, θ and θ' usually denote directions on the sphere S^{d-1}. When applying a reparameterization f (a neural network), the expectation should be over the base measure (often uniform on the sphere, denoted as σ_{d-1}). While the context clearly implies the intent is to compute the expectation over the uniform distribution on the sphere for both variables, the notation |f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')| inside the expectation is mathematically inconsistent because the expectation is indexed only by θ and θ'. A more precise notation would be E_{θ, θ' ∼ σ_{d-1}} [|f(θ)^⊤ f(θ')|]. As written, it uses the subscript σ_{d-1} once but looks as if it might imply independent samples from that measure. It is a minor notation ambiguity that a reader can resolve, but it is technically malformed.

comments.title: Implicit assumption on the domain of f
comments.quote: where f is a class of all Borel measurable functions from S^{d-1} to S^{d-1}
comments.explanation: The text states f is a 'class' of functions, but in the preceding formula (2), the supremum is taken over 'f'. If f is a class, the supremum should be over the functions within that class. The phrasing is slightly imprecise as it conflates the function itself with the space of functions. Given the subsequent sentence 'We parameterize f ... by using a deep neural network', the authors clearly mean the set of functions represented by the architecture. This is a common shortcut in ML papers, but slightly formally loose.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'admissible constant'
comments.explanation: In section Section 3.1, the authors define C > 0 as admissible if the set M_C is non-empty. Later, they state 'By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1'. While true for E[|θᵀθ'|], this assumes the measure σ is such that the expectation of the absolute inner product is bounded by the max of |θᵀθ'| (which is 1). This is trivially true, but the paper does not explicitly clarify if C can range up to 1 or if specific choices of C are required for specific d. It's a minor clarification point since the constraint E[|θᵀθ'|] ≤ C is always satisfied for C=1, but it might leave a user wondering what happens if they set C > 1.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Definition of Dual Form Regularization
comments.explanation: In Section 1 (Key Equations) and Section 3.1, the Alignment Regularization is defined as E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] ≤ C. However, in Definition 2 (Section 3.2), the dual form introduces the term - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|]. The context states λC is equivalent to the inverse of C. In standard Lagrange multiplier theory for a constraint f(θ,θ') ≤ C, the penalty should be λ(E[|θ⊤θ'|] - C). By minimizing the negative of the objective (or maximizing a Lagrangian), the constant term λC*C would appear. The current formulation - λ_C E_{θ,θ'∼σ} [|θ^⊤ θ'|] omits the constant offset λC*C. While common in some machine learning literature for 'regularization', strictly speaking, it is not the dual form of the constrained problem defined in 3.1 unless the constant is accounted for or the definition of 'dual form' is interpreted loosely as a penalized objective.

comments.title: Notation confusion in Reparameterized Dual Form
comments.explanation: In the summary and Section 3.2, the index θ∼σ_{d-1} appears, where σ_{d-1} is defined in the summary as the 'uniform distribution'. However, the symbol σ_{d-1} is not clearly defined in the 'Notation' subsection of the text itself. While the context allows one to infer σ_{d-1} denotes the uniform measure on the sphere, the notation is inconsistent with the earlier defined σ (a probability measure to be optimized) and S^{d-1} (the unit sphere). This might confuse a reader whether σ_{d-1} refers to a distribution or the sphere itself. It is likely a typo for the uniform measure which should be denoted using the standard Haar measure notation or clearly mapped to S^{d-1}.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper tackles a well-motivated and timely problem in optimal transport for machine learning by proposing the Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distance, which elegantly reframes the SW–Max-SW computational-quality trade-off as an optimization over probability measures on the unit sphere. The conceptual motivation is strong, and the writing is generally clear and logically structured, effectively positioning the work within the sliced OT literature and articulating a compelling high-level vision. However, the most significant issue at this stage is the under-specified technical roadmap: the excerpt lacks sufficient detail on how the optimal projection distribution is parameterized, optimized, and made differentiable for end-to-end generative modeling, nor does it address the computational and convergence challenges of learning a regularized distribution over a high-dimensional manifold. Additionally, the claim that DSW recovers Max-SW as a special case requires careful theoretical justification, as extracting a non-convex, point-mass solution from a regularized distributional objective is non-trivial and not yet substantiated. To elevate the paper, the authors should sharpen the exposition around the optimization algorithm (e.g., particle-based vs. parametric families), provide preliminary guarantees on tractability or convergence, and clarify how the diversity regularization is balanced against transport cost without introducing optimization bias or numerical instability.

comments.title: Inverted/corrupted cosine similarity formula in Notation
comments.explanation: The formula for cosine similarity is presented in a structurally inverted or OCR-corrupted state. The intended definition is clearly $\cos(\theta, \theta') = \frac{\theta^\top \theta'}{\|\theta\| \|\theta'\|}$. As rendered, it implies the reciprocal of the standard cosine definition. Given the context of projecting onto a unit sphere and subsequent reliance on angular measures, the authors certainly intend the standard formula. This is a typesetting/OCR artifact, but it should be corrected to prevent mathematical confusion.

comments.title: Terminology slip: Radon transform integrates over 'lines' instead of hyperplanes
comments.quote: The _Radon transform_ (RT) maps a function _I ∈_ L[1] (R _[d]_ ) to the space of functions defined over space of lines in R _[d]_ .
comments.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^d$ for $d \geq 2$, the Radon transform integrates over hyperplanes (codimension-1 affine subspaces), parameterized by a normal vector $\theta \in S^{d-1}$ and offset $t \in \mathbb{R}$. Referring to these integrals as 'lines' is only accurate for the special case $d=2$. The mathematical definition provided immediately after correctly describes integration over hyperplanes/slices. This is a minor terminology slip that does not impact the mathematical derivations, but clarifying it as 'hyperplanes' or 'slices' would improve precision.

comments.title: Missing normalization constant in Sliced-Wasserstein definition
comments.explanation: The integral over the unit sphere $S^{d-1}$ lacks the normalization constant $1/\text{Vol}(S^{d-1})$ (or explicit expectation notation $\mathbb{E}_{\theta \sim \text{Unif}}$). While this is a widely adopted convention in machine learning literature (often absorbed into algorithmic constants or implied by subsequent Monte Carlo averaging steps), strictly speaking, it changes the scale of the distance by a dimension-dependent factor. The authors should either include the normalization factor for theoretical rigor or explicitly state that they are using an unnormalized integral for computational convenience, as this affects how $C$ and other constants scale across dimensions.

comments.title: Obscured integration limits in 1D Wasserstein closed-form
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction drops the upper bound of the integral. The standard closed-form expression for 1D Wasserstein distance is $W_p(\mu, \nu) = \left( \int_0^1 |F^{-1}_\mu(z) - F^{-1}_\nu(z)|^p dz \right)^{1/p}$. The text shows a '0' at the start but misses the '1' at the upper limit. This is a clear OCR artifact, and the intended result is obvious from context. I recommend verifying the final PDF rendering to ensure the limits are visible.

comments.title: Incorrect threshold for metric equivalence with Max-SW
comments.quote: As a consequence, when p ≥ 1 and C ≥ 1/d, DSWp(·, ·; C), SWp, maxSWp, and Wp are topologically equivalent, namely, the convergence of probability measures under DSWp(·, ·; C) implies the convergence of these measures under other metrics and vice versa.
comments.explanation: The authors claim topological equivalence holds for C ≥ 1/d. However, the Dirac measure required to recover the Max-SW distance yields an expected inner product of E_{θ,θ'∼δ_θ}|θ^⊤θ'| = |θ^⊤θ| = 1. Thus, Dirac measures only belong to M_C when C ≥ 1. For 1/d ≤ C < 1 (which applies for all d > 1), the feasible set strictly excludes Dirac measures, meaning the DSW objective will strictly underperform Max-SW and the metrics cannot be equivalent. Furthermore, the expected absolute inner product of two random unit vectors in R^d scales as O(1/√d), not 1/d. The threshold appears to be a typo and should be C ≥ 1 to correctly recover Max-SW and establish the claimed equivalence. This misstatement affects a key theoretical guarantee regarding the method's behavior at different regularization strengths.

comments.title: Misattributed bound in Cauchy-Schwarz argument
comments.quote: By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, _C_ is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere.
comments.explanation: The phrasing incorrectly implies that the parameter C itself is bounded above by 1. In reality, C is a user-defined hyperparameter. It is the random variable |θ^⊤θ'| that is bounded by 1 via Cauchy-Schwarz (since ∥θ∥=∥θ′∥=1), which means its expectation is bounded by 1. The authors likely meant to state that the maximum possible value of the constraint's left-hand side is 1, which implies that M_C becomes the set of all probability measures whenever C ≥ 1. The current wording creates a misleading implication about the admissible range of C and conflates the tunable parameter with the geometric bound on the expectation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in DSW projection set between main text and figure caption
comments.quote: vary the number of projections _N ∈_ _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _}_ for the DSW. ... Each dot of SW and DSW corresponds to the number of projections chosen in _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _,_ 10[4] _}_ .
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that DSW converges efficiently with fewer projections than uniform slicing methods, and they appropriately adjust the DSW experimental set to omit the largest value ($10^4$). However, the figure caption explicitly lists $10^4$ as a tested value for DSW alongside SW, creating a direct contradiction with the methodology description in the main text. A careful reader might assume either a missing experimental result for DSW at $N=10^4$, or a typographical error in the caption. This is a localized inconsistency that does not undermine the paper but warrants correction for reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified comparison of per-minibatch computational speed
comments.quote: DSW-10 achieves better model quality than Max-GSW-NN and Max-SW and is one order-of-magnitude faster than these sliced distances.
comments.explanation: The authors claim DSW-10 is roughly 10× faster per minibatch than Max-SW, despite DSW-10 evaluating 10 projections while Max-SW evaluates only 1. In standard optimal transport and slicing frameworks, increasing the number of projections monotonically increases per-minibatch compute cost (due to sorting and W1 calculations per slice). Max-SW's per-iteration cost is typically low unless an expensive inner optimization loop is used to find the best direction each epoch. The text does not clarify the batch sizes, epoch budgets, or whether Max-SW employs iterative direction optimization during training. Without this context, the speed claim is difficult to reconcile with standard computational complexity expectations. I acknowledge that DSW's parameterized directional distribution $f_\phi$ allows a single forward pass to estimate the expectation, which can be highly optimized, but the lack of methodological transparency leaves the empirical speed advantage somewhat ambiguous.

comments.title: Slight logical leap in interpreting aggregate metric $A_N$ as pairwise orthogonality
comments.quote: When $\lambda_C = 1000$, $A_N$ is close to 0.1, suggesting that all projected directions are nearly orthogonal.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly define $A_N = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i,j=1}^N |\theta_i^\top \theta_j|$ and note that for $N=10$, $A_N \approx 0.1$ aligns with the diagonal terms contributing $10/100 = 0.1$. This mathematically implies the off-diagonal terms (pairwise similarities) average near zero. However, the statement 'suggesting that all projected directions are nearly orthogonal' extrapolates from an aggregate expectation to a deterministic property of the finite sample set. In high-dimensional spaces, random directions are naturally near-orthogonal, and a low sample average does not guarantee every pair is orthogonal (some could be correlated while others cancel out). The claim is directionally sound and consistent with the paper's regularization goal, but the justification skips the intermediate step of explicitly separating diagonal/off-diagonal contributions. A minor clarification would strengthen the statistical rigor of this observation.

comments.title: Confusing phrasing regarding the Cauchy-Schwarz bound on the regularization constant
comments.quote: By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere.
comments.explanation: The authors intend to convey that the expected inner product E_{θ,θ′∼σ}[|θ⊤θ′|] is bounded above by 1 due to the unit norm of directions and Cauchy-Schwarz. Consequently, choosing C ≥ 1 makes the constraint E[|θ⊤θ′|] ≤ C trivially satisfied by all probability measures. The phrasing 'C is no greater than 1' incorrectly suggests that the admissible constant C itself is bounded by 1, rather than the *expectation* that is bounded. This is a minor wording imprecision that does not affect the mathematical correctness of the set definitions or the subsequent logic, but could momentarily confuse a careful reader about the role of C.

comments.title: Dual form objective omits the standard Lagrange multiplier constant term
comments.quote: there exists a non-negative constant λC depending on C such that the dual form of DSW distance takes the following form _where_ M _denotes the space of all probability measures on the unit sphere_ S _[d][−]_[1] _._
comments.explanation: In standard Lagrangian duality for the primal problem sup_{σ ∈ M_C} E_σ[W], the Lagrangian dual objective typically includes the constant term +λ_C C (arising from relaxing the linear constraint E[|θ⊤θ′|] - C ≤ 0). The authors' Definition 2 defines DSW*_p without this constant. While this omission does not affect the location of the optimal measure σ* (since the constant vanishes when taking the supremum over σ) and they correctly state the weak duality inequality (DSW ≥ DSW*), strictly speaking the defined dual objective is shifted by -λ_C C relative to the standard Lagrangian dual function. In practice, the authors treat λ_C as a free tuning hyperparameter to control angular spread rather than deriving it exactly from C via complementary slackness, so this has no practical impact on optimization or results. However, it remains a minor technical inaccuracy in the formal duality formulation.

This misstatement affects a key theoretical guarantee regarding the method's behavior at different regularization strengths.

comments.title: Misattributed bound in Cauchy-Schwarz argument
comments.quote: By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, _C_ is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere.
comments.explanation: The phrasing incorrectly implies that the parameter C itself is bounded above by 1. In reality, C is a user-defined hyperparameter. It is the random variable |θ^⊤θ'| that is bounded by 1 via Cauchy-Schwarz (since ∥θ∥=∥θ′∥=1), which means its expectation is bounded by 1. The authors likely meant to state that the maximum possible value of the constraint's left-hand side is 1, which implies that M_C becomes the set of all probability measures whenever C ≥ 1. The current wording creates a misleading implication about the admissible range of C and conflates the tunable parameter with the geometric bound on the expectation.

comments.title: Inconsistency in DSW projection set between main text and figure caption
comments.quote: vary the number of projections _N ∈_ _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _}_ for the DSW. ... Each dot of SW and DSW corresponds to the number of projections chosen in _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 10[2] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[2] _,_ 10[3] _,_ 5 _×_ 10[3] _,_ 10[4] _}_ .
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that DSW converges efficiently with fewer projections than uniform slicing methods, and they appropriately adjust the DSW experimental set to omit the largest value ($10^4$). However, the figure caption explicitly lists $10^4$ as a tested value for DSW alongside SW, creating a direct contradiction with the methodology description in the main text. A careful reader might assume either a missing experimental result for DSW at $N=10^4$, or a typographical error in the caption. This is a localized inconsistency that does not undermine the paper but warrants correction for reproducibility.

comments.title: Underspecified comparison of per-minibatch computational speed
comments.quote: DSW-10 achieves better model quality than Max-GSW-NN and Max-SW and is one order-of-magnitude faster than these sliced distances.
comments.explanation: The authors claim DSW-10 is roughly 10× faster per minibatch than Max-SW, despite DSW-10 evaluating 10 projections while Max-SW evaluates only 1. In standard optimal transport and slicing frameworks, increasing the number of projections monotonically increases per-minibatch compute cost (due to sorting and W1 calculations per slice). Max-SW's per-iteration cost is typically low unless an expensive inner optimization loop is used to find the best direction each epoch. The text does not clarify the batch sizes, epoch budgets, or whether Max-SW employs iterative direction optimization during training. Without this context, the speed claim is difficult to reconcile with standard computational complexity expectations. I acknowledge that DSW's parameterized directional distribution $f_\phi$ allows a single forward pass to estimate the expectation, which can be highly optimized, but the lack of methodological transparency leaves the empirical speed advantage somewhat ambiguous.

comments.title: Slight logical leap in interpreting aggregate metric $A_N$ as pairwise orthogonality
comments.quote: When $\lambda_C = 1000$, $A_N$ is close to 0.1, suggesting that all projected directions are nearly orthogonal.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly define $A_N = \frac{1}{N^2} \sum_{i,j=1}^N |\theta_i^\top \theta_j|$ and note that for $N=10$, $A_N \approx 0.1$ aligns with the diagonal terms contributing $10/100 = 0.1$. This mathematically implies the off-diagonal terms (pairwise similarities) average near zero. However, the statement 'suggesting that all projected directions are nearly orthogonal' extrapolates from an aggregate expectation to a deterministic property of the finite sample set. In high-dimensional spaces, random directions are naturally near-orthogonal, and a low sample average does not guarantee every pair is orthogonal (some could be correlated while others cancel out). The claim is directionally sound and consistent with the paper's regularization goal, but the justification skips the intermediate step of explicitly separating diagonal/off-diagonal contributions. A minor clarification would strengthen the statistical rigor of this observation.

comments.title: Confusing phrasing regarding the Cauchy-Schwarz bound on the regularization constant
comments.quote: By Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, C is no greater than 1, thus M1 contains all probability measures on the unit sphere.
comments.explanation: The authors intend to convey that the expected inner product E_{θ,θ′∼σ}[|θ⊤θ′|] is bounded above by 1 due to the unit norm of directions and Cauchy-Schwarz. Consequently, choosing C ≥ 1 makes the constraint E[|θ⊤θ′|] ≤ C trivially satisfied by all probability measures. The phrasing 'C is no greater than 1' incorrectly suggests that the admissible constant C itself is bounded by 1, rather than the *expectation* that is bounded. This is a minor wording imprecision that does not affect the mathematical correctness of the set definitions or the subsequent logic, but could momentarily confuse a careful reader about the role of C.

comments.title: Dual form objective omits the standard Lagrange multiplier constant term
comments.quote: there exists a non-negative constant λC depending on C such that the dual form of DSW distance takes the following form _where_ M _denotes the space of all probability measures on the unit sphere_ S _[d][−]_[1] _._
comments.explanation: In standard Lagrangian duality for the primal problem sup_{σ ∈ M_C} E_σ[W], the Lagrangian dual objective typically includes the constant term +λ_C C (arising from relaxing the linear constraint E[|θ⊤θ′|] - C ≤ 0). The authors' Definition 2 defines DSW*_p without this constant. While this omission does not affect the location of the optimal measure σ* (since the constant vanishes when taking the supremum over σ) and they correctly state the weak duality inequality (DSW ≥ DSW*), strictly speaking the defined dual objective is shifted by -λ_C C relative to the standard Lagrangian dual function. In practice, the authors treat λ_C as a free tuning hyperparameter to control angular spread rather than deriving it exactly from C via complementary slackness, so this has no practical impact on optimization or results. However, it remains a minor technical inaccuracy in the formal duality formulation.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper introduces Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) distance, a well-motivated generalization of both SW and Max-SW that learns an optimal distribution over projection directions, regularized to encourage diversity. The writing is generally clear and the motivation—balancing exploration of informative directions with computational efficiency—is compelling. However, several concerns affect the paper's quality and clarity. First, the abstract and introduction do not clearly specify the form of the regularization or how the optimal distribution is learned in practice (e.g., parametric vs. non-parametric, optimization method), which is critical for reproducibility. Second, the claim that DSW "can be computed efficiently" is vague; the computational complexity relative to SW and Max-SW is not discussed, and the trade-off between the cost of optimizing over distributions and the savings from fewer projections is not analyzed. Third, while the paper notes that DSW recovers Max-SW as a special case under no regularization, it does not clarify whether DSW strictly improves upon SW in high-dimensional regimes or under which conditions the learned distribution yields a better approximation of the true Wasserstein distance. Finally, the empirical section is referenced but not described in the provided text, so it is impossible to assess whether the experiments convincingly demonstrate superiority over baselines. Overall, the paper presents a promising idea, but a more precise problem definition, computational analysis, and clearer theoretical guarantees would significantly strengthen the contribution.

comments.title: Domain mismatch in generalized Radon transform definition
comments.quote: GI(t,θ) := ∫_{R^d} I(x) δ(t - g(x,θ)) dx, where t∈R and θ∈Ω_θ. Here, Ω_θ is a compact subset of R^d and g: R^d × S^{d-1} → R is a defining function
comments.explanation: The text states that the second argument of g lies in S^{d-1} (the unit sphere), but immediately above it says that θ is taken from Ω_θ, a compact subset of R^d. These are contradictory. The intended meaning is almost certainly that g is defined on R^d × Ω_θ, since the generalized Radon transform allows a parameter set that is not necessarily the sphere. The confusion is compounded when the passage later says that the standard Radon transform corresponds to the special case where Ω_θ = S^{d-1} and g(x,θ)=⟨x,θ⟩. A reader could reasonably wonder whether the domain of g is always the sphere or varies with the parameter set. This is a definitional error that should be corrected for clarity.

comments.title: Non-standard definition of L^p(R^d)
comments.quote: For any p ≥ 1, L^p(R^d) is the set of real-valued functions on R^d with finite p-th moment.
comments.explanation: Standard notation L^p(R^d) denotes functions whose |f|^p is Lebesgue integrable, not functions with finite p-th moment. The p-th moment is a property of probability measures (e.g., ∫|x|^p dµ(x)), not of functions. The authors likely intended to refer to the space of probability measures with finite p-th moment, but that notation (P_p(R^d)) is introduced later in Section 2.1. Using L^p in this non-standard way is confusing and unnecessary, though it does not affect the core content of the paper since this notation is not used later in the provided passage.

comments.title: Stray '1' before Wasserstein closed-form expression
comments.quote: has a closed-form expression 1 W_p(µ,ν) = (∫_0^1 |F_µ^{-1}(z) - F_ν^{-1}(z)|^p dz)^{1/p}
comments.explanation: There is an unexpected '1' immediately before 'W_p(µ,ν)'. This appears to be an OCR artifact, possibly from an equation number or a corrupt character. If left in the text, a reader might misinterpret it as a coefficient or as part of a formula. The authors should remove it. This is a minor formatting issue, but it could cause momentary confusion.

comments.title: Ambiguous metric for λ_C selection
comments.quote: for _λC_ in DSW (see Definition 2), it is chosen in the set _{_ 1 _,_ 10 _,_ 100 _,_ 1000 _}_ such that its Wasserstein-2 (FID score) (between 10000 random generated images and all images from corresponding validation set) is the lowest among the four values.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'Wasserstein-2 (FID score)' is ambiguous. Earlier in the same paragraph, the authors state that for MNIST they evaluate using Wasserstein-2 distance, while for other large datasets they use FID score. The parenthetical suggests that 'Wasserstein-2' and 'FID score' are being used interchangeably or that both metrics are used, which could confuse a careful reader. In reality, λ_C is selected using the same metric as the evaluation for each dataset: Wasserstein-2 for MNIST and FID for the others. The sentence would be clearer if it explicitly said 'its Wasserstein-2 score (on MNIST) or its FID score (on other datasets) is the lowest'.

comments.title: Misleading complexity claim for empirical Wasserstein-2 distance
comments.quote: it is expensive to compute empirical Wasserstein-2 distance as its complexity is of order _O_ ( _k_[2] log _k_ ) where _k_ is the number of support points.
comments.explanation: The stated complexity O(k^2 log k) for the empirical Wasserstein-2 distance is imprecise without specifying the algorithm. Exact Wasserstein-2 distance between two empirical distributions of size k typically requires O(k^3) (e.g., Hungarian algorithm) or O(k^3 log k) (network simplex). The O(k^2 log k) figure is more appropriate for approximate algorithms such as the Sinkhorn algorithm with entropic regularization, but the authors do not mention using any approximation. Given that the passage is only justifying why they resort to FID for large datasets, this inaccuracy is minor, but it could mislead readers about the computational burden of exact computation.
