progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The manuscript presents a theoretically motivated extension to Sliced-Wasserstein distances by introducing the Distributional Sliced-Wasserstein (DSW) metric. The introduction is effective in articulating the trade-off between the high sample complexity of standard Sliced-Wasserstein and the informativeness loss of Max-Sliced-Wasserstein, thereby establishing a strong rationale for the proposed method. The core contribution—the conceptual shift from sampling directions to optimizing a probability distribution over the unit sphere—promises a rigorous solution to the variance-bias dilemma in distribution estimation. While the writing is clear, the text cuts off immediately before defining the mathematical formulation. A critical area for future evaluation will be the specific regularizing constraints used to encourage distinct directions; the authors must demonstrate that the optimization over the distribution of directions is computationally tractable and that the resulting distance maintains the desirable statistical properties of Wasserstein distances. The reader is looking forward to the precise definition of DSW and proofs establishing its generalization properties to verify the theoretical claims.

comments.title: Missing/Incoherent Definition of DSW Distance
comments.quote: Given two probability measures µ and ν on R^d with finite p-th moments where p ≥ 1 and an admissible regularizing constant C > 0 . The distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance (DSW) of order p between µ and ν is given by: [section 2] _where R is the Radon transform operator._ The DSW aims to find the optimal probability measure of slices on the unit sphere S_{d-1}.
comments.explanation: Definition 1 is missing the actual mathematical formula (integral or optimization problem) for the DSW distance. Furthermore, a sentence fragment '_where R is the Radon transform operator._' appears to be an artifact from the previous section that was erroneously merged into the definition, breaking the continuity of the text and making it difficult to follow the mathematical formulation.

comments.title: Ambiguous use of 'no regularization'
comments.quote: In the case of no regularization, our proposed method recovers max-(generalized) SW as a special case.
comments.explanation: The term 'no regularization' is not explicitly defined in the Abstract or Introduction. While the reader may infer from the later text that this refers to $C=1$, the claim lacks grounding in the prose present in the early sections, making it ambiguous to a reader before reaching the mathematical formulation in Section 3.1.

comments.title: Misleading terminology in reference
comments.quote: Paty & Cuturi (2019) extended the idea of the max-sliced distance to the max-subspace distance
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the seminal work 'Min-Sliced Wasserstein' but name the resulting distance 'max-subspace'. This conflates the 'Min' approach (finding the best separation) with a 'Max' variant, which is confusing for readers familiar with the literature.

comments.title: OCR/Notation artifact in Complexity
comments.explanation: The big-O notation for computational complexity is corrupted by an OCR error, appearing as '$k_{[2]}$' instead of '$k^2$'. This makes the formula difficult to read and could be misinterpreted mathematically.

comments.title: Corrupted Notation in Notation Section
comments.explanation: The equation defining the cosine of the angle between vectors is corrupted by OCR noise (garbled brackets and symbols), rendering it unintelligible despite the context suggesting it refers to the inner product on the unit sphere.

comments.title: Inconsistent logic regarding computational efficiency
comments.quote: On all these large datasets, DSW outperforms all the other baselines in both FID score of the learned model and computational efficiency. ... For example, on CIFAR10, with the same computational time, FID scores of DSW are always lower than those of SW about 20 units. ... DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60.
comments.explanation: The authors claim 'computational efficiency' by comparing DSW at $N=100$ projections to SW at $N=10000$ projections. While the example shows DSW yields better FID scores with fewer resources, it does not compare results 'with the same computational time' as implied by the efficiency claim. The 46 vs 60 example likely represents a parameter sweep comparison, not an iso-time comparison, creating a logical mismatch.

comments.title: Spelling error in Figure Caption
comments.quote: Two Guassians
comments.explanation: The word 'Gaussians' (plural of Gaussian) is misspelled as 'Guassians' in the figure caption. This is a minor typographical error that detracts from the manuscript's polish.

comments.title: Grammatical error in Theorem statement
comments.quote: DSWp (·, · ; C), SWp, maxSWp, and Wp are topological equivalent
comments.explanation: The adverb 'topologically' is missing. Since multiple metrics are being described, the correct phrasing is 'are topologically equivalent'. The current phrasing is grammatically incorrect and awkward.

While they did extend the concept of finding discriminative projections to subspaces, naming the resulting distance 'max-subspace' is confusing and inconsistent with the standard terminology of their paper. It conflates the 'Min-Sliced' approach (finding the best separation/minimal distance) with a 'Max' variant, potentially confusing a reader familiar with the literature.

comments.title: OCR/Notation artifact
comments.explanation: The formatting indicates an OCR error. The subscript '[2]' corresponds to the exponent '2' intended for the big-O notation describing computational complexity (likely $O(k^2)$). This makes the formula difficult to read and could be misinterpreted mathematically if the user assumes 'k_2' is a variable.

comments.title: Missing Definition of DSW Distance
comments.quote: Given two probability measures µ and ν on R^d with finite p-th moments where p ≥ 1 and an admissible regularizing constant C > 0 . The distributional sliced-Wasserstein distance (DSW) of order p between µ and ν is given by:
comments.explanation: The passage explicitly states that Definition 1 gives the DSW distance 'is given by:', but the actual mathematical formula (integral or optimization problem) is missing from the provided text. The following text fragment '[section 2] where R is the Radon transform operator.' appears to be an artifact from the previous section (likely the end of the Radon Transform definition). Without the definition of DSW, it is impossible to verify the mathematical formulation, the incorporation of the regularization term, or the claim that DSW is a generalization of Max-SW and SW. The reader cannot proceed to the analysis or experiment sections with a clear understanding of the method.

comments.title: Misplaced Text Fragment in Definition 1
comments.quote: [section 2] _where R is the Radon transform operator._ The DSW aims to find the optimal probability measure of slices on the unit sphere S_{d-1}.
comments.explanation: The phrase '_where R is the Radon transform operator._' appears in the middle of the DSW discussion paragraph, likely as an artifact from the OCR process or a formatting error where the end of the Radon Transform definition (2.2) was accidentally merged into the start of the DSW definition (3.1). The Radon Transform definition typically reads, 'RI(t, θ) := ... dx', but here it appears as a standalone sentence between the preamble and the definition. This breaks the flow and clarity of the definition section.

comments.title: Corrupted Notation in Notation Section
comments.explanation: This equation is severely corrupted by OCR noise, appearing as nonsense symbols like '[⊤]', '[′][′]', and nested brackets. The context clarifies that this defines the cosine of the angle between two vectors on the unit sphere, which is typically the Euclidean inner product ⟨θ, θ′⟩ (since for unit vectors cos θ = ⟨θ, θ′⟩). While the meaning is inferable, the notation is unintelligible as presented.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding 'computational efficiency' vs specific data point comparison
comments.quote: On all these large datasets, DSW outperforms all the other baselines in both FID score of the learned model and computational efficiency. ... For example, on CIFAR10, with the same computational time, FID scores of DSW are always lower than those of SW about 20 units. ... It is interesting to note that on these high-dimensional datasets, Max-SW performs rather poorly: ... DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60.
comments.explanation: The passage first claims DSW outperforms other methods in 'computational efficiency,' defining this by stating that 'with the same computational time,' DSW's FID is about 20 units lower. This describes a comparison of points along the time-axis of a plot (i.e., iso-time comparison). However, the subsequent specific example ('DSW can achieve an FID score of 46 while SW with 10000 projections still has a worse FID score of over 60') compares specific data points with vastly different numbers of projections ($N=100$ vs. $N=10000$). If DSW is computationally more efficient (faster), then the computation time for SW with 10000 projections would likely be significantly longer than the time for DSW with 100 projections. Therefore, the 46 vs 60 comparison likely refers to a parameter sweep comparison (fixed $N$), not a 'same computational time' comparison. Referencing this specific data point to support the general claim about 'computational efficiency' creates a logical mismatch, as the example may not represent the 'same time' condition previously established. A reader might infer that the 46/60 gap holds true for same-time runs, even though the authors likely meant to illustrate the quality advantage with fewer resources.

comments.title: Spelling Error in Figure Caption
comments.quote: Two Guassians
comments.explanation: In the figure caption text, the word 'Gaussians' (plural of Gaussian) is misspelled as 'Guassians'. This is a minor typographical error. While not a technical flaw, it can be distracting for readers and lower the perceived polish of the manuscript. The intended meaning is clearly to refer to two bivariate Gaussian distributions.

comments.title: Singular vs. Plural Adjective Usage
comments.quote: DSWp (·, · ; C), SWp, maxSWp, and Wp are topological equivalent
comments.explanation: In Theorem 2, the authors state that four different metrics are 'topological equivalent'. Grammatically, the adverb 'topologically' is required to modify the adjective 'equivalent' when describing multiple subjects (e.g., 'are topologically equivalent'). 'Topological equivalent' is singular and semantically awkward in this context.
